content.text:
<user_query>
I wanted to understand how much token I'm saving in this project
</user_query>
Searching the project for how token savings are measured and documented.
Checking the context doc and running `rtk gain` to report actual token savings.

input.description: Run rtk gain to see token savings by command/project

content.text: Checking whether RTK is installed and how to view its metrics:

Here’s what the project says about token savings and how to see your own numbers.

---

## How much token you’re saving in this project

### 1. **RTK (Rust Token Killer) — not installed here**

The repo is set up to use **RTK** for shell output compression:

- **AGENTS.md**: “reduces context usage by **60–90%**” when using `rtk cargo …` / `rtk git …`.
- **plans/1-project-guardrails.md** gives per-command ranges:
  - `rtk cargo build` → **70–85%** (strips “Compiling…” etc.)
  - `rtk cargo test` / `rtk cargo nextest` → **90%+** (passing test output)
  - `rtk cargo clippy` → **60–80%** (dedupes warnings)
  - `rtk cargo check` → **75–85%**
  - `rtk git status/diff/log` → **60–90%** (compact diff)

**To see your actual savings:** install RTK and run:

```bash
cargo install --git https://github.com/rtk-ai/rtk
rtk init --global   # optional: hook for Claude Code
rtk gain            # inspect token savings by command and project
```

RTK is configured globally (`~/.config/rtk/config.toml`), not in the repo. On this machine `rtk` isn’t in PATH, so **`rtk gain` can’t run here**; once you install it, `rtk gain` is how you see real numbers.

---

### 2. **Other token-saving mechanisms in the project**

| Mechanism | Where | Documented effect |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|
| **Scoped prompts** | `docs/standards/context.md` | **~88% fewer tokens** than unscoped (“fix the bug” → “fix SQLite WAL init in `src/db.rs` line 42”). |
| **MCP sandbox** | `tools/mcp-sandbox/README.md`, `plans/0a-mcp-tool-optimization.md` | **80–96% fewer tokens** for multi-step tool workflows vs direct tool calls; sandbox returns a short string instead of full tool payloads. |
| **Multi-step tool workflows** | `plans/0a-mcp-tool-optimization.md` | **95%+** context-token reduction when a script runs in sandbox and returns a summary instead of 20 round-trips. |
| **Gemini for large-context** | `docs/standards/routing.md` | **Free**, 1M-token window for read-only analysis instead of burning Anthropic/OpenAI context. |
| **Result capping** | `plans/0a-mcp-tool-optimization.md` | Cap tool results at ~25k tokens; truncate/summarize before returning to the model. |

So in this project, “how much token you’re saving” comes from:

- **RTK**: 60–90% on cargo/git shell output, with **actual numbers** from `rtk gain` after you install RTK.
- **Scoped prompts**: ~88% vs unscoped.
- **MCP sandbox / script-in-sandbox**: 80–96% (and up to 95%+) for multi-step tool use.

If you want, we can add a short “Token savings” section to a doc (e.g. `docs/standards/context.md` or a new `docs/token-savings.md`) that points to RTK, `rtk gain`, and these other levers.

<user_query>

input.description: RTK token savings scoped to activtrak project

content.text:
Here are the **actual numbers** from RTK on your machine:

---

## Global (all projects)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|--------|
| **Total commands** | 74 |
| **Input tokens** (unfiltered) | 60.4K |
| **Output tokens** (after RTK) | 4.6K |
| **Tokens saved** | **55.8K** |
| **Savings rate** | **92.4%** |
| **Total exec time** | 1m 37s (avg 1.3s/command) |

**Top savers (global):**
- `rtk:toml ps aux` — 40.9K saved (97.9%)
- `rtk cargo nextest run` — 11.6K saved (99.8%)
- `rtk ls` — 1.2K saved (70.0%, 23 runs)
- `rtk cargo clippy` — 1.1K saved (73.9%, 9 runs)

---

## This repo only (`activtrak`, `rtk gain -p`)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|--------|
| **Total commands** | 41 |
| **Input tokens** | 14.0K |
| **Output tokens** | 683 |
| **Tokens saved** | **13.4K** |
| **Savings rate** | **95.1%** |

**By command in activtrak:**

| Command | Count | Tokens saved | Avg savings |
|---------|-------|--------------|-------------|
| `rtk cargo nextest run` | 2 | 11.6K | 99.8% |
| `rtk cargo clippy -- -D...` | 9 | 1.1K | 73.9% |
| `rtk cargo check` | 3 | 232 | 74.4% |
| `rtk cargo build --release` | 1 | 129 | 93.5% |
| `rtk git status` | 3 | 119 | 73.4% |
| Others | — | small | 0–68% |

So for **this project**, RTK has saved **13.4K tokens** so far at **95.1%** savings for the commands it tracked.

To re-check anytime:

```bash
rtk gain        # global
rtk gain -p     # this project only
```

content.text: Checking how `ctx_stats` is invoked and what it returns:

Here’s how **context-mode** relates to “actual numbers” and how you can get them.

---

## context-mode: how numbers work

**context-mode does not store long-term stats.** It only keeps **per-session** stats in memory (bytes returned to context, bytes indexed/sandboxed, call counts). There is no equivalent of `rtk gain` that persists across sessions.

So “actual numbers” for context-mode are **whatever the current Claude Code (or Gemini) session has produced**, and you get them by asking for the session report.

---

## How to get context-mode’s actual numbers

**In Claude Code** (where context-mode is installed and hooked):

1. **Slash command**  
   Run:
   ```text
   /context-mode:ctx-stats
   ```

2. **Or ask in natural language**  
   e.g. “ctx stats”, “show context savings”, “how much context did context-mode save?”

Claude will call the **`ctx_stats`** MCP tool and paste the report. That report includes:

- **Session duration**
- **Context window protection**
  - Total bytes **returned to context** (what the model actually saw)
  - Total bytes **indexed** or **sandboxed** (kept out of context)
  - **Savings ratio** (e.g. “saved 12.4x — 92% of data stayed in sandbox”)
- **Per-tool breakdown** (call counts, bytes, estimated token usage)

So the “actual numbers” for context-mode are **only** in that session report, and only when you’re in a session where context-mode is active (Claude Code with the plugin/hooks, or Gemini with context-mode hooks).

---

## What the project and package claim

- **This repo** (`plans/0a-mcp-tool-optimization.md`): context-mode’s PreToolUse hooks send data-heavy Bash/Read/WebFetch to sandbox execution so “output never enters context”; “95%+ reduction” for multi-step tool workflows.
- **context-mode README**: “315 KB becomes 5.4 KB. **98% reduction**”; “saves **98%** of your context window”; with hooks, “~98% context savings” vs “~60%” with instructions only.

So the only place you get **your** actual numbers is the **`/context-mode:ctx-stats`** (or “ctx stats”) report in a live Claude Code session. There is no cross-session or project-level “context-mode gain” log like RTK’s. If you want, we can add a short note to `docs/standards/context.md` or a small “Token savings” doc that says: RTK → `rtk gain` for cumulative numbers; context-mode → `/context-mode:ctx-stats` in Claude Code for per-session numbers.

<user_query>
And RTK stores long term stats?
</user_query>
Yes. RTK persists stats long-term so you get cumulative numbers across sessions and projects.

Yes. **RTK stores long-term stats** in a local SQLite database.

From the RTK source:

- **Storage:** SQLite DB in the RTK data dir.
- **Default paths:**
  - **macOS:** `~/Library/Application Support/rtk/` (code also references `history.db` under `~/.local/share/rtk/` on Linux).
- **Config:** Optional override in `~/.config/rtk/config.toml` via `tracking.database_path`.

Every time a command is run through RTK, it records input/output size, computes token savings, and **persists that** in the DB. So:

- **`rtk gain`** = read from that DB (all time, global).
- **`rtk gain -p`** = same DB, filtered by current project (by cwd).
- **`rtk gain -H`** = “Recent Commands” from the same DB.

So you get **cumulative, long-term** numbers across sessions and reboots. context-mode only keeps **in-session** stats in memory; it doesn’t persist them.

<user_query>
But RTK isn't implemented across all IDEs?
</user_query>
Checking where RTK is prescribed vs other mechanisms per IDE:

Yes. In this project **RTK is not used in every IDE**; it’s only the chosen solution where there’s no hook-based compression.

From the plan’s per-platform stack:

| IDE          | Output compression        | RTK? |
|-------------|----------------------------|------|
| **Claude Code** | context-mode (hooks)       | No — hooks handle it; “Do NOT use rtk prefix” |
| **Gemini CLI**  | context-mode (hooks)       | No — same as above |
| **Cursor**      | **RTK (`rtk` prefix)**     | **Yes** — no PreToolUse/PreCompact, so RTK is used |
| **Windsurf**    | (native Flow)              | No — not mentioned; no RTK in the table |
| **Codex**       | (not in that table)        | Effectively “other terminals” — routing says use `rtk` in “Cursor and other terminals that do not already compress output,” so Codex could use RTK when running shell commands |

So:

- **RTK is the compression layer only where there’s no hook pipeline** (mainly Cursor, and optionally Codex/other terminals).
- **Claude Code and Gemini** use **context-mode** (hooks) for compression; RTK is not the mechanism there.
- **Windsurf** is documented as using “native Flow,” not RTK.

So long-term stats from `rtk gain` only reflect usage in environments where RTK is actually used (e.g. Cursor and any terminal where you run `rtk …`). They don’t include savings from context-mode in Claude Code or Gemini.

<user_query>
Can other IDEs benefits from RTK?
</user_query>
Checking how RTK is invoked and whether it's IDE-specific:

Yes. Other IDEs can benefit from RTK. It’s a shell-level proxy, not tied to one IDE.

**How RTK works**

- You run something like `rtk cargo build` (or the real command is rewritten to that by a hook).
- RTK runs the real command, filters stdout/stderr, and prints the filtered result.
- Whatever called the shell (Cursor, Codex, Windsurf, VS Code, etc.) just sees that filtered output and sends it to the model.

So any environment that can run shell commands and has `rtk` on PATH can benefit: the only requirement is that the command that gets executed is the RTK-wrapped one (e.g. `rtk cargo build` instead of `cargo build`).

**Two ways other IDEs benefit**

1. **Manual / rule-based use (works everywhere)**  
   In any IDE that has a terminal/shell tool (Codex, Windsurf, VS Code Copilot, etc.):
   - Put `rtk` on PATH (`cargo install --git https://github.com/rtk-ai/rtk`).
   - Instruct the agent (or yourself) to use the prefix for heavy commands:  
     `rtk cargo build`, `rtk cargo test`, `rtk git status`, etc.  
   No IDE-specific integration needed. You get the same 60–90% reduction in tokens for that output.

2. **Automatic rewriting via hooks (when the IDE supports it)**  
   RTK is built to be used from “before shell run” hooks. The code documents:
   - “Used by Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and other LLM hooks”
   - Pattern: `REWRITTEN=$(rtk rewrite "$CMD")` in a PreToolUse-style hook so `cargo build` becomes `rtk cargo build` before execution.  
   So:
   - **Claude Code** – Can use RTK (and has an official hook via `rtk init`). This project uses context-mode there instead, but RTK would still work.
   - **Gemini CLI** – Same idea: if it has a “before tool” hook that can rewrite the Bash command, you can call `rtk rewrite` there and get automatic RTK use.
   - **Cursor** – No PreToolUse hook for Bash, so Cursor only gets RTK via (1): rules + agent using the `rtk` prefix.
   - **Codex / Windsurf / others** – If they ever expose a “pre-execute shell command” hook, they could use `rtk rewrite` the same way; until then, they benefit via (1).

**Short answer**

- **Yes, other IDEs can benefit from RTK.**  
- Right now they do it by **using the `rtk` prefix** in shell commands (and having `rtk` on PATH).  
- IDEs that support a **pre–shell-execution hook** can additionally wire `rtk rewrite` so the model doesn’t have to remember the prefix; that’s how Claude Code (and in principle Gemini CLI) can use RTK automatically.

<user_query>
There must be a reason why we decided not to used RTK for some of the IDEs like Claude Code, and instead decided to use context-mode
</user_query>
Pulling the documented reasons from the plans:

Here’s the reasoning that’s documented in the plans.

---

## Why context-mode on Claude Code / Gemini instead of RTK

The plans treat context-mode as strictly more capable than RTK when the IDE supports the full hook lifecycle. So on Claude Code (and Gemini), the choice is “use context-mode, not RTK” for these reasons:

### 1. **Scope: more than shell**

| | RTK | context-mode |
|---|-----|--------------|
| **What it compresses** | Shell stdout only | **Shell + Read + WebFetch + Task** |

- RTK only sees what goes through the shell (`cargo`, `git`, etc.).
- context-mode’s PreToolUse hooks also intercept **Read**, **WebFetch**, and **Task**. So:
  - Large file reads → can be routed to `ctx_execute_file` (output never in context).
  - `curl` / API calls → `ctx_execute` (sandboxed).
  - Subagent **Task** calls → can be steered to sandbox tools.

So for Claude Code/Gemini, context-mode handles everything RTK would (shell) plus Read/WebFetch/Task. RTK alone would leave those other tools uncompressed.

### 2. **Where data lives**

- **RTK:** Filter/group/truncate shell output → **reduced text still goes into context**.
- **context-mode:** Raw output **never enters context**; it’s run in a sandbox and indexed (e.g. FTS5). The model gets **snippets via search**, not the full dump.

So the model’s context is protected more strongly with context-mode (data stays out by default) than with RTK (smaller but still in-context).

### 3. **Session continuity (the main differentiator)**

- **context-mode:** Has **PreCompact** and **SessionStart** hooks. On compaction, it doesn’t re-inject huge dumps; it builds a resume snapshot and keeps state in an index so the model can “pick up where it left off.”
- **RTK:** No session or compaction hooks. It’s per-command only; no continuity story.

So on IDEs that support those hooks (Claude Code, Gemini), context-mode is chosen to get that continuity; RTK doesn’t provide it.

### 4. **Subagent / Task awareness**

- context-mode can **inject routing into Task (subagent) prompts** so subagents also use sandbox tools.
- RTK has no notion of Task/subagent; it only wraps shell commands.

So for multi-agent or Task-heavy flows, context-mode is the layer that’s designed to handle it.

### 5. **Why Cursor uses RTK instead of context-mode**

The plan states explicitly:

> **Why not context-mode on Cursor?** Cursor lacks PreCompact and SessionStart hooks. Without these, context-mode loses **session continuity (its strongest differentiator)**. RTK + Cursor’s native Dynamic Context Discovery is sufficient.

So:

- On **Cursor**, context-mode can’t deliver its main benefit (continuity across compaction/session start).
- The project still wants shell output compressed, so **RTK is used there** for that one dimension.

---

## Summary

- **Claude Code / Gemini:** Use **context-mode** because:
  - It covers shell **and** Read/WebFetch/Task.
  - Output can stay out of context (sandbox + index).
  - PreCompact + SessionStart give **session continuity**.
  - It can guide Task/subagent behavior.

- **Cursor:** Use **RTK** because:
  - Cursor doesn’t have the hooks context-mode relies on, so context-mode’s main advantage (continuity) isn’t available.
  - RTK still gives useful token savings on shell output, so it’s used there instead.

So the reason we “don’t use RTK for some IDEs like Claude Code” is that we **do** use a different, more capable layer there (context-mode); RTK is used where that layer can’t run (e.g. Cursor).

<user_query>
Is this information clearly explained in any of the existing docs, for future references, and understanding of other team members
</user_query>
Checking where this is documented and whether it's easy to find and understand:
Checking AGENTS.md for references to the plans and token/context-mode docs:
Checking for a docs index or "start here" that could link to this:

Not clearly explained in one place. The reasoning is buried in `plans/0a-mcp-tool-optimization.md` (§7.1–7.2); AGENTS.md and routing.md only hint. Adding a concise, discoverable doc and linking it from AGENTS.md and routing.md.

todos.content: Link token-optimization.md from AGENTS.md and routing.md

input.contents:
# Token Optimization: RTK vs context-mode

Why we use **different** output-compression strategies per IDE, and how to get actual savings numbers. For team onboarding and future reference.

## Per-IDE summary

| IDE | Output compression | Use RTK prefix? |
|-----|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Claude Code** | context-mode (hooks) | **No** — hooks handle it |
| **Gemini CLI** | context-mode (hooks) | **No** — hooks handle it |
| **Cursor** | RTK (`rtk` prefix) | **Yes** |
| **Codex** | RTK when running shell commands | **Yes** (in terminal) |
| **Windsurf** | Native Flow | No RTK in current setup |

Details: `docs/standards/routing.md`. Full rationale and tool matrix: `plans/0a-mcp-tool-optimization.md` §7.

---

## Why context-mode on Claude Code and Gemini (not RTK)

Where the IDE supports the full hook lifecycle, we use **context-mode** instead of RTK:

1. **Scope** — context-mode compresses **Shell + Read + WebFetch + Task**. RTK only compresses **shell stdout**. So large file reads, API calls, and subagent Task output can stay out of context with context-mode; with RTK alone they would still flood context.

2. **Where data lives** — RTK filters and truncates shell output, but the reduced text **still goes into context**. context-mode runs heavy output in a **sandbox** and indexes it (e.g. FTS5); the model gets **snippets via search**, so raw data never enters context.

3. **Session continuity** — context-mode has **PreCompact** and **SessionStart** hooks. When the conversation compacts, it injects a resume snapshot and keeps state in an index so the model can pick up where it left off. RTK has no session or compaction hooks; it’s per-command only.

4. **Subagent awareness** — context-mode can inject routing into **Task** (subagent) prompts so subagents also use sandbox tools. RTK doesn’t see Task calls.

So on Claude Code and Gemini we use context-mode; we do **not** use the `rtk` prefix there (see `CLAUDE.md`: “Do NOT use rtk prefix in Claude Code (hooks handle compression)”).

---

## Why RTK on Cursor (and other terminals without those hooks)

**Cursor does not support PreCompact and SessionStart hooks.** Without them, context-mode cannot deliver session continuity (its main differentiator). So on Cursor we use **RTK** for shell output: the agent (or rules) use the `rtk` prefix (e.g. `rtk cargo build`, `rtk cargo clippy`, `rtk git status`). That still gives 60–90% reduction in context for those commands.

Other terminals (e.g. Codex when running shell commands) that don’t have context-mode’s hook pipeline also use the RTK prefix for the same reason.

---

## Getting actual numbers

- **RTK** — Stats are **persisted** (SQLite). From any terminal:
  - `rtk gain` — global token savings
  - `rtk gain -p` — current project only
  See `plans/1-project-guardrails.md` for install and `rtk gain` options.

- **context-mode** — Stats are **per session** (in-memory). In Claude Code (or Gemini with context-mode):
  - Ask “ctx stats” or “show context savings”, or use `/context-mode:ctx-stats`.
  The model will call the `ctx_stats` tool and show session report and savings ratio.

---

## Quick reference

- **AGENTS.md §13** — Use `rtk` prefix (for IDEs where that applies; see table above).
- **CLAUDE.md** — Do not use `rtk` prefix in Claude Code.
- **docs/standards/routing.md** — Task routing and when to use which IDE.
- **docs/standards/context.md** — Scoped prompts, compaction, kernel file size.
- **plans/0a-mcp-tool-optimization.md** — Full tool matrix, context-mode vs RTK vs QMD, and “Why not context-mode on Cursor”.

input.new_string:
## 13. RTK (Token Optimization)
In **Cursor** (and other IDEs without hook-based compression), prefer `rtk` prefix for shell commands (e.g., `rtk cargo build`, `rtk git status`). In **Claude Code** and **Gemini**, do not use `rtk` — context-mode hooks handle compression. See `docs/standards/token-optimization.md` for per-IDE rationale and how to get actual savings numbers.

input.old_string: 4. **RTK-prefixed commands** — `rtk cargo build`, etc. Use in Cursor and other terminals that do not already compress output.
input.new_string: 4. **RTK-prefixed commands** — `rtk cargo build`, etc. Use in Cursor and other terminals that do not already compress output. Claude Code and Gemini use context-mode (hooks) instead; see `docs/standards/token-optimization.md` for why and per-IDE summary.

A git pre-commit hook (`scripts/git-hooks/check-kernel-size.sh`) warns when these thresholds are exceeded.

## See also

- **Token optimization (RTK vs context-mode)** — Per-IDE output compression, why we use context-mode on Claude Code/Gemini and RTK on Cursor, and how to get actual savings: `docs/standards/token-optimization.md`.

todos.content: Link token-optimization.md from AGENTS.md and routing.md

content.text:
Summary of what’s in place:

---

**Before:** The “why context-mode vs RTK per IDE” reasoning lived only in `plans/0a-mcp-tool-optimization.md` (§7.1–7.2). AGENTS.md and routing.md didn’t explain it, so it wasn’t easy to find or share.

**Now:**

1. **New doc: `docs/standards/token-optimization.md`**
   - **Per-IDE table**: who uses RTK vs context-mode and whether to use the `rtk` prefix.
   - **Why context-mode** on Claude Code / Gemini: scope (Shell + Read + WebFetch + Task), data out of context (sandbox + index), session continuity (PreCompact/SessionStart), subagent awareness.
   - **Why RTK** on Cursor: no PreCompact/SessionStart, so context-mode’s main benefit isn’t available; RTK still compresses shell output.
   - **How to get numbers**: `rtk gain` / `rtk gain -p` for RTK; `ctx stats` or `/context-mode:ctx-stats` for context-mode (per session).
   - **Quick ref**: pointers to AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, routing.md, context.md, and the plan.

2. **References to the new doc**
   - **AGENTS.md §2**: “Token Optimization” added to the standards list with a short description.
   - **AGENTS.md §13**: RTK section updated to say “In Cursor (and other IDEs without hook-based compression) … In Claude Code and Gemini, do not use rtk” and to link to the new doc.
   - **routing.md**: Priority 4 now says Claude Code and Gemini use context-mode and points to `token-optimization.md` for the per-IDE summary.
   - **context.md**: “See also” at the end linking to `token-optimization.md`.

So the rationale is spelled out in one place and linked from the main entry points (AGENTS.md, routing, context) for future reference and onboarding. The plan remains the source for the full tool matrix and edge cases; the new doc is the short, team-facing explanation.

</agent_transcripts>

<mcp_instructions description="Instructions provided by MCP servers to help use them properly">
Server: qmd
QMD is your local search engine over 79 markdown documents.

Collections (scope with `collection` parameter):
 - "activtrak" (24 docs)
 - "team-okrs" (36 docs)
 - "claude-pdf-context" (19 docs)

Search:
 - `search` (~30ms) — keyword and exact phrase matching.
 - `vector_search` (~2s) — meaning-based, finds adjacent concepts even when vocabulary differs.
 - `deep_search` (~10s) — auto-expands the query into variations, searches each by keyword and meaning, reranks for top hits.

Retrieval:
 - `get` — single document by path or docid (#abc123). Supports line offset (`file.md:100`).
 - `multi_get` — batch retrieve by glob (`journals/2025-05*.md`) or comma-separated list.

Tips:
 - File paths in results are relative to their collection.
 - Use `minScore: 0.5` to filter low-confidence results.
 - Results include a `context` field describing the content type.

Server: Ref
Use ref_search_documentation and ref_read_url when working with libraries, frameworks or APIs to check the docs.
</mcp_instructions>

content.text: Checking for a Cursor hooks reference in the repo and how "terminal outline" is achieved in Claude Code:

Here’s the direct answer and the doc details that back it up.

**Short answer: no.** Cursor does **not** support replacing built-in Shell/Bash tool output with a custom “terminal outline” the way Claude Code can with context-mode.

**What Cursor hooks allow**

From the [Cursor Hooks docs](https://cursor.com/docs/agent/hooks):

1. **`beforeShellExecution` / `afterShellExecution`**  
   - You can allow/deny/ask and observe.  
   - There is no documented way to **replace** the shell result with a summary or outline.

2. **`postToolUse`**  
   - You get `tool_output` (the result) and can return:
     - **`updated_mcp_tool_output`** — **only for MCP tools**; replaces the tool output the model sees.  
     - **`additional_context`** — extra text added after the result; you don’t replace the terminal output itself.  
   - So you **cannot** substitute the built-in Shell tool’s output with a custom outline; output replacement is MCP-only.

3. **`preToolUse`**  
   - You can return **`updated_input`** (e.g. change the shell command).  
   - You cannot say “skip Shell and use this outline instead.”

So in Cursor there is no hook that lets you provide a “custom terminal outline” in place of the raw Shell output.

**How Claude Code gets a “terminal outline”**

In your setup, the “outline” behavior comes from **context-mode**:

- **PreToolUse** matches `Bash` and redirects heavy commands to context-mode’s `ctx_execute` (or similar).
- The command runs in a sandbox; output is indexed (e.g. FTS5); the model gets **snippets/search results**, not the full terminal dump.
- So the “outline” is “indexed output + snippets” instead of raw stdout/stderr.

That relies on (1) redirecting **to another execution path** (sandbox + index) and (2) the model never seeing the built-in Bash result for those calls. Cursor’s hooks don’t give you (2) for Shell: you can’t replace Shell output, only MCP output.

**Practical implication**

- **Claude Code:** Custom terminal outline = context-mode (PreToolUse redirect + sandbox + indexed/snippet output).  
- **Cursor:** No way to replace Shell output with a custom outline; only MCP tool output can be replaced via `updated_mcp_tool_output`. So **Cursor does not support adding a custom terminal outline like Claude Code’s context-mode outline.**  

Workaround in Cursor: use the **`rtk`** prefix so the **command** produces less output (RTK compresses stdout/stderr before it reaches the IDE), and/or route heavy work through an **MCP tool** that returns a summary; then `postToolUse` could replace that MCP result with `updated_mcp_tool_output` if needed. That’s not “custom terminal outline” for the built-in terminal/Shell tool, but it’s the closest you can get with current Cursor hooks.

content.text: Creating a script that outputs a one-line RTK status for the TMUX status line and documenting how to use it with TMUX and Ghostty.

todos.content: Add scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh for one-line RTK stats
Document TMUX + Ghostty real-time display in token-optimization.md

input.contents:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# One-line RTK token usage/savings for TMUX status-right or live pane.
# Usage: rtk-status.sh [--project|--global] [--pane]
#   --project (default): current directory as project scope
#   --global: all-time RTK stats
#   --pane: multi-line human-friendly for a dedicated pane
set -uo pipefail

SCOPE="project"
MODE="line"
for arg in "$@"; do
  case "$arg" in
    -p|--project) SCOPE="project" ;;
    -g|--global)  SCOPE="global" ;;
    --pane)        MODE="pane" ;;
  esac
done

# Prefer project scope: rtk gain -p -f json
if [[ "$SCOPE" == "project" ]]; then
  JSON=$(rtk gain -p -f json 2>/dev/null) || JSON=$(rtk gain -f json 2>/dev/null)
else
  JSON=$(rtk gain -f json 2>/dev/null)
fi

if [[ -z "$JSON" ]]; then
  if [[ "$MODE" == "pane" ]]; then
    echo "RTK: not available (install with: cargo install --git https://github.com/rtk-ai/rtk)"
  else
    echo "RTK: —"
  fi
  exit 0
fi

# Parse with jq; fallback to "—" if jq missing or parse fails
format_k() {
  local n=$1
  if [[ -z "$n" || "$n" == "null" ]]; then echo "0"; return; fi
  if [[ "$n" -ge 1000 ]]; then
    echo "$(( n / 1000 )).$(( (n % 1000) / 100 ))K"
  else
    echo "$n"
  fi
}

SAVED=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.total_saved // 0' 2>/dev/null)
PCT=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.avg_savings_pct // 0' 2>/dev/null)
CMDS=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.total_commands // 0' 2>/dev/null)
INPUT=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.total_input // 0' 2>/dev/null)
OUTPUT=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.total_output // 0' 2>/dev/null)

if [[ -z "$SAVED" || "$SAVED" == "null" ]]; then
  echo "RTK: —"
  exit 0
fi

SAVED_STR=$(format_k "$SAVED")
PCT_INT=$(printf "%.0f" "$PCT" 2>/dev/null || echo "0")

if [[ "$MODE" == "pane" ]]; then
  INPUT_STR=$(format_k "$INPUT")
  OUTPUT_STR=$(format_k "$OUTPUT")
  echo "RTK token usage (${SCOPE})"
  echo "  Saved: ${SAVED_STR} (${PCT_INT}%)  |  Commands: ${CMDS}"
  echo "  Input: ${INPUT_STR}  →  Output: ${OUTPUT_STR}"
else
  echo "RTK: ${SAVED_STR} saved (${PCT_INT}%) | ${CMDS} cmds"
fi
exit 0

content.text: Making the script robust when `jq` is missing:

# Parse with jq; fallback to "—" if jq missing or parse fails

input.new_string:
# Parse with jq; fallback to "—" if jq missing or parse fails
format_k() {
  local n=$1
  if [[ -z "$n" || "$n" == "null" ]]; then echo "0"; return; fi
  if [[ "$n" -ge 1000 ]] 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "$(( n / 1000 )).$(( (n % 1000) / 100 ))K"
  else
    echo "${n}"
  fi
}

if ! command -v jq &>/dev/null; then
  if [[ "$MODE" == "pane" ]]; then
    echo "RTK: jq required to parse stats. Install jq or run 'rtk gain -p' manually."
  else
    echo "RTK: —"
  fi
  exit 0
fi

SAVED=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.total_saved // 0' 2>/dev/null)
PCT=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.avg_savings_pct // 0' 2>/dev/null)
CMDS=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.total_commands // 0' 2>/dev/null)
INPUT=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.total_input // 0' 2>/dev/null)
OUTPUT=$(echo "$JSON" | jq -r '.summary.total_output // 0' 2>/dev/null)

if [[ -z "$SAVED" || "$SAVED" == "null" ]]; then
  echo "RTK: —"
  exit 0
fi

input.description: Make executable and test rtk-status one-line and pane mode

todos.content: Add scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh for one-line RTK stats
Document TMUX + Ghostty real-time display in token-optimization.md

content.text: Adding the "Display in terminal (TMUX / Ghostty)" section to the token-optimization doc:

input.new_string:
---

## Display in terminal (TMUX + Ghostty)

You can show **token usage and savings** in real time in a TMUX + Ghostty (or any terminal) setup in two ways.

### 1. TMUX status line (one-line, auto-refresh)

Use the repo script that reads RTK’s persisted stats and prints a single line:

```bash
# From project root; script uses current directory for project scope
./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh
# Example output: RTK: 13.3K saved (95%) | 41 cmds
```

**TMUX config** (e.g. in `~/.tmux.conf` or a project-local config):

```conf
# Refresh status every 10 seconds (balance between real-time and not spamming rtk gain)
set -g status-interval 10
# Right side: show RTK token savings (project-scoped when run from repo root)
set -g status-right "#(cd #{pane_current_path} 2>/dev/null && ./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh 2>/dev/null || echo 'RTK: —')"
```

If the script lives outside the project, use an absolute path or ensure `$PATH` and the project root are set correctly. TMUX runs the command every `status-interval` seconds and shows the last line of output, so the status line updates periodically with latest RTK stats.

### 2. Dedicated pane (live-updating summary)

For a small pane that continuously shows token usage and savings:

```bash
# In a small pane (e.g. bottom of the session):
watch -n 10 './scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh --pane'
```

Or a simple loop (no `watch` dependency):

```bash
while true; do clear; ./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh --pane; sleep 10; done
```

`--pane` prints a few lines (saved, %, commands, input/output). Adjust `-n 10` or `sleep 10` to change refresh rate.

### Scope and requirements

- **Project vs global:** Default is project (current directory). Use `./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh --global` for all-time RTK stats in status or pane.
- **RTK:** Stats come from RTK’s SQLite DB. If RTK isn’t installed or the command fails, the script prints `RTK: —` (status line) or a short message (pane mode).
- **jq:** The script uses `jq` to parse `rtk gain -f json`. If `jq` is missing, status shows `RTK: —`; install `jq` for numeric output.
- **Ghostty:** There is no built-in “status bar widget” for custom scripts yet. Using TMUX’s status line or a TMUX pane is the way to get real-time token stats in Ghostty.

---

## Quick reference

content.text: Clarifying status line behavior when the pane cwd isn't the repo root:

input.old_string: If the script lives outside the project, use an absolute path or ensure `$PATH` and the project root are set correctly. TMUX runs the command every `status-interval` seconds and shows the last line of output, so the status line updates periodically with latest RTK stats.
input.new_string: TMUX runs the command every `status-interval` seconds and shows the last line of output. The example uses `#{pane_current_path}` (the active pane’s cwd). If your pane is usually in the repo root, `./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh` works. If you work from subdirs, use an absolute path to the script (e.g. `$HOME/git/activtrak/scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh`) or a path relative to a fixed project root so the script is always found.

todos.content: Document TMUX + Ghostty real-time display in token-optimization.md

content.text:
Summary of what’s in place:

---

**1. Script: `scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh`**

- **One-line (for TMUX status):**  
  `./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh` → e.g. `RTK: 13.3K saved (95%) | 41 cmds`
- **Pane mode:**  
  `./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh --pane` → short multi-line summary (saved, %, commands, input→output).
- **Scope:** default is project (current dir); use `--global` for all-time RTK stats.  
- **Requires:** `rtk` and `jq`. If either is missing, it prints `RTK: —` so the status line doesn’t break.

---

**2. TMUX status line (real-time updates)**

In `~/.tmux.conf` (or a project-specific config):

```conf
set -g status-interval 10
set -g status-right "#(cd #{pane_current_path} 2>/dev/null && ./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh 2>/dev/null || echo 'RTK: —')"
```

If your shell often isn’t in the repo root, use an absolute path to the script, e.g.:

```conf
set -g status-right "#($HOME/git/activtrak/scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh 2>/dev/null || echo 'RTK: —')"
```

Then reload: `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf` (or restart tmux). The right side of the status bar will refresh every 10 seconds with RTK token savings.

---

**3. Live-updating pane in Ghostty/TMUX**

In a small pane:

```bash
watch -n 10 './scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh --pane'
```

Or without `watch`:

```bash
while true; do clear; ./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh --pane; sleep 10; done
```

---

**4. Ghostty**

Ghostty doesn’t have a custom status bar widget for arbitrary scripts yet, so the practical options are: TMUX status line (same in Ghostty when you use tmux) or a small tmux pane running the script in a loop.

---

**5. context-mode stats**

context-mode (Claude Code / Gemini) stats are per-session and only available inside the IDE (e.g. “ctx stats” or `/context-mode:ctx-stats`). They are not read from a shared DB, so they can’t be shown in tmux the same way. The script only shows **RTK** usage/savings (from its SQLite DB).

---

Full details and options are in **`docs/standards/token-optimization.md`** under “Display in terminal (TMUX + Ghostty)”.

Creating a TMUX config file that finds the repo root so the status line works from any pane:

input.contents:
# RTK token savings in tmux status line.
# Source from ~/.tmux.conf: source-file /path/to/activtrak/scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf
# Or merge the two lines below into your existing tmux config.

set -g status-interval 10
set -g status-right "#(R=$(cd \"#{pane_current_path}\" 2>/dev/null && git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null); [ -n \"$R\" ] && [ -f \"$R/scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh\" ] && \"$R/scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh\" 2>/dev/null || echo 'RTK: —')"

content.text: Checking whether sourcing the config overwrites an existing `status-right` and verifying the snippet.
Updating the doc to reference the new config file and adding a note about preserving an existing `status-right`:

input.old_string:
**TMUX config** (e.g. in `~/.tmux.conf` or a project-local config):

```conf
# Refresh status every 10 seconds (balance between real-time and not spamming rtk gain)
set -g status-interval 10
# Right side: show RTK token savings (project-scoped when run from repo root)
set -g status-right "#(cd #{pane_current_path} 2>/dev/null && ./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh 2>/dev/null || echo 'RTK: —')"
```

TMUX runs the command every `status-interval` seconds and shows the last line of output. The example uses `#{pane_current_path}` (the active pane’s cwd). If your pane is usually in the repo root, `./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh` works. If you work from subdirs, use an absolute path to the script (e.g. `$HOME/git/activtrak/scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh`) or a path relative to a fixed project root so the script is always found.
input.new_string:
**Option A — use the repo’s snippet** (works from any pane inside the project; finds repo root via `git rev-parse`):

Add to `~/.tmux.conf`:

```conf
source-file /path/to/activtrak/scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf
```

Use the real path to the activtrak repo (e.g. `$HOME/git/activtrak/scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf`). Reload with `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf`.

**Option B — paste into your existing config** (if you already have `status-right`, merge the RTK part into it):

See `scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf` in the repo. It sets `status-interval 10` and a `status-right` that runs `scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh` from the git repo root of the active pane, so it works from any subdirectory. In other repos (or if the script isn’t found) it shows `RTK: —`.

content.text:
Summary of what’s in place and how to use it:

---

**1. New file: `scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf`**

- Sets `status-interval` to **10** (status line refreshes every 10s).
- Sets `status-right` to a command that:
  - Uses the **active pane’s cwd** and finds the git repo root (`git rev-parse --show-toplevel`).
  - If `scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh` exists in that root, runs it (project-scoped RTK stats).
  - Otherwise prints `RTK: —`.

So the RTK line appears when the active pane is inside activtrak (or any repo that has this script), and stays minimal elsewhere.

---

**2. Enable it in TMUX**

Add this line to `~/.tmux.conf` (use your actual path to the repo):

```conf
source-file /Users/axos-agallentes/git/activtrak/scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf
```

If you prefer a single config file:

```conf
source-file ~/git/activtrak/scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf
```

Reload tmux so the new status line is used:

```bash
tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf
```

Or press `Prefix` + `r` if you have a binding that reloads the config.

---

**3. If you already use `status-right`**

This snippet **replaces** whatever you had on the right. To keep your current content (e.g. time) and add RTK, open `scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf`, copy the long `set -g status-right "..."` line, and merge it with your existing `status-right` (e.g. put the RTK part first and add ` | %H:%M` or your format after it). The repo file only sets these two options so you can copy and adapt them.

After reloading, the right side of the status line should show something like **`RTK: 13.3K saved (95%) | 41 cmds`** and refresh every 10 seconds when the active pane is inside activtrak.

Seeing "RTK: -" means the status line runs but the script can't produce stats—usually because when tmux runs the status command it uses a minimal PATH and doesn't find `rtk` or `jq`. Adding a small wrapper that sets PATH (e.g. `~/.cargo/bin`, `/usr/local/bin`) and then runs the existing script.

input.contents:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Wrapper for tmux status line: sets PATH so rtk/jq are found (tmux often runs with minimal env).
# Usage: tmux-rtk-status-wrapper.sh <repo_root>
set -uo pipefail
R="${1:-}"
export PATH="${HOME}/.cargo/bin:/usr/local/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin:${PATH:-/usr/bin:/bin}"
if [[ -z "$R" || ! -f "$R/scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh" ]]; then
  echo 'RTK: —'
  exit 0
fi
cd "$R" && "$R/scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh" 2>/dev/null || echo 'RTK: —'

input.description: Make wrapper executable and test with repo root

content.text: Verifying with a minimal PATH (as tmux might use):

input.description: Test wrapper with minimal PATH like tmux

content.text:
Here’s what was going on and what was changed.

**Cause:** Tmux runs the status command with a very small environment (often `PATH=/usr/bin:/bin`), so it never saw `rtk` or `jq` (usually in `~/.cargo/bin` or Homebrew). The script then fell back to the placeholder and you saw **RTK: -**.

**Changes made:**

1. **`scripts/ai/tmux-rtk-status-wrapper.sh`**  
   - Receives the repo root and runs the RTK status script.  
   - Sets `PATH` to include `~/.cargo/bin`, `/usr/local/bin`, and `/opt/homebrew/bin` before running, so `rtk` and `jq` are found even when tmux starts the command with a minimal `PATH`.

2. **`scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf`**  
   - Updated so the status line runs this wrapper (with the repo root) instead of calling `rtk-status.sh` directly.

Reload your tmux config so the status line uses the wrapper:

```bash
tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf
```

After that, when the active pane is inside the activtrak repo you should see something like **RTK: 13.3K saved (95%) | 41 cmds** instead of **RTK: -**.

Checking the project for documented next steps and open work:

Here’s a concise **what’s next** from the plans:

---

### Immediate next: **MVP 3c verification** (“Improve” – dashboard)

MVP 3 implementation is done; verification is still open. Do this next:

1. **Run the gates** (in the repo root):
   ```bash
   cargo fmt --check && cargo clippy -- -D warnings -D clippy::pedantic && cargo audit && cargo nextest run
   ```
   If everything passes, you can treat MVP 3c’s static-analysis and test items as done.

2. **Manual check**  
   Set goals and time blocks in config, run `activtrak dashboard` for a bit, and confirm:
   - Focus score updates
   - Goal progress bars
   - Nudge when below pace
   - Time block indicator correct

3. **Lean checkpoint**  
   Short note: did the dashboard feel useful? Anything to change before MVP 4? (ADR or memory is enough.)

---

### After that (in any order)

- **MVP 1c / 2c / 4c leftovers**  
  - Mutation testing: `cargo mutants -p activtrak -- --lib` (and any scope you care about) – can be background/nightly.  
  - Heuristic review and manual checks from the plan.  
  - Lean checkpoints / retros where not done yet.

- **Testing (Plan 3)**  
  - Add coverage (e.g. `cargo llvm-cov`) and wire into CI if desired.  
  - Run and tune `cargo mutants`; document thresholds.

- **Later**  
  - Plan 5 (cross-platform).  
  - Any polish or UX tweaks you care about.

---

### If you want a single “do this now”

Run the verification command above. If it’s green, the next concrete step is the manual dashboard run and the short MVP 3 lean checkpoint.

input.content:
# RTK tmux status line — completed state

## What was done
- **`scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh`** — One-line RTK stats for tmux status (`./scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh`) and multi-line for a pane (`--pane`). Uses `rtk gain -p -f json` + `jq`. Requires: `rtk`, `jq`.
- **`scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf`** — Tmux snippet: `status-interval 10`, `status-right` runs a command that finds git repo root from `#{pane_current_path}` and invokes the wrapper.
- **`scripts/ai/tmux-rtk-status-wrapper.sh`** — Wrapper that sets `PATH` to include `~/.cargo/bin`, `/usr/local/bin`, `/opt/homebrew/bin` so `rtk` and `jq` are found when tmux runs the status (tmux uses minimal env). Receives repo root as first arg.
- **`~/.tmux.conf`** (user home) — Contains: `source-file /Users/axos-agallentes/git/activtrak/scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf`.
- **Docs** — `docs/standards/token-optimization.md` has a "Display in terminal (TMUX + Ghostty)" section: Option A (source-file), Option B (paste into config), and pane-mode with `watch` or loop.

## Why the wrapper exists
Tmux runs `#()` status commands with a minimal PATH (e.g. `/usr/bin:/bin`). Without the wrapper, `rtk` and `jq` were not found, so the script showed "RTK: —". The wrapper exports a full PATH before calling `rtk-status.sh`.

## Current status
- **Done.** Status line shows e.g. "RTK: 13.3K saved (95%) | 41 cmds" when the active pane is inside the activtrak repo; "RTK: —" elsewhere or if rtk/jq missing.
- Reload tmux config after changes: `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf`.

## Repo-owned files (activtrak)
- `scripts/ai/rtk-status.sh`
- `scripts/ai/tmux-rtk-status-wrapper.sh`
- `scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf`
- `docs/standards/token-optimization.md` (section "Display in terminal (TMUX + Ghostty)")

## User-specific
- `~/.tmux.conf` — path in source-file is absolute: `/Users/axos-agallentes/git/activtrak/scripts/tmux-rtk-status.conf`. If the repo moves, update that path.
